verdict_reasoning: KLK2 (kallikrein-related peptidase 2) is a serine protease canonically produced and secreted by the prostate. The protein is detected in serum as a soluble protease, particularly elevated in prostate cancer. However, KLK2 is a single-pass transmembrane precursor (pre-pro form) that undergoes regulated proteolytic cleavage to release the soluble active enzyme. Cell-surface biotinylation and surface-proteomics studies in prostate cancer cells document KLK2 on the cell surface; the membrane-anchored TM precursor stage is the source of the shed serum form. Additionally, KLK2 is implicated in cancer-cell ecto-enzyme activity and is part of the broader kallikrein family, many members of which exhibit cancer-associated ecto-presentation. The dominant biological form detected in serum is soluble, but the TM precursor stage that feeds shedding is documented on the cell surface, qualifying this as a dual_localization / transient TM precursor of a shed-ligand-dominant gene. Surface-directed CAR-T and antibody programs targeting KLK2 engage the protein on prostate cancer cells, further supporting at least contextual surface accessibility.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Unclear whether the TM precursor stage is stable enough on the cell surface to warrant `yes` (classical_surface_receptor) or whether the transient / short-lived TM stage with rapid shedding to a dominant soluble pool places it more precisely in `contextual` / `dual_localization`. Surface-proteomics quantification of TM vs. soluble ratio and kinetics of TM→shed conversion would resolve this.
